article_title: Falcon Heavy Returns After 560 Days to Close Out the Troubled ViaSat-3 Constellation
summary: Mostly accurate, but three corrections needed: April 28 backup window is not in the Viasat press release, center core serial B1098 is not in the cited Spaceflight Now snapshot, and 'Landing Zone 40' is a known Spaceflight Now error (LZ-40 does not exist; the actual SpaceX RTLS pads at the Cape are LZ-1 and LZ-2).

findings.message: Misattribution: 'Viasat has cited a backup window on Tuesday, April 28' — the April 20 Viasat press release does not mention any backup window
findings.details: The GlobeNewswire-carried Viasat press release (source-0) confirms the April 27 primary date, the 85-minute window, and the 10:21 a.m. EDT open, but contains no reference to an April 28 backup. Either find a source that names this backup window or remove the sentence.

findings.message: Unsourced fact: Center core serial number 'B1098' is not in the Spaceflight Now launch schedule snapshot
findings.details: Source-1 (Spaceflight Now) confirms B1072 (2nd flight) and B1075 (22nd flight) for the side boosters and that the center core 'will be expended', but does not state the center core's tail number. The article reports it as 'B1098' — that detail is unsourced. Either find a source that lists B1098 explicitly or replace with 'the center core' without the serial number.

findings.message: 'Landing Zones 40 and 2' inherits a Spaceflight Now error — there is no LZ-40 at the Cape
findings.details: Source-1 verbatim says 'Landing Zone 2 and Landing Zone 40' but this is a known typo: SLC-40 is a launch pad (the Falcon 9 SLC-40), not a landing zone. The actual SpaceX RTLS landing zones at Cape Canaveral SFS are LZ-1 and LZ-2. The article correctly transcribes the source but inherits the factual error. Recommendation: write 'Landing Zones 1 and 2' (correct) and consider noting the source typo, or use a less specific phrasing such as 'the Cape's two SpaceX landing zones'.

Viasat's press release lists customers as 'commercial mobility, fixed services and defense'. The article paraphrases as 'commercial mobility, fixed-broadband, and defense'. Close enough that this is informational only, but for tighter source fidelity consider matching the source's wording.

content_preview.title: Falcon Heavy Returns After 560 Days to Close Out the Troubled ViaSat-3 Constellation
content_preview.summary: SpaceX targets April 27 to launch ViaSat-3 F3 to the Asia-Pacific, ending Falcon Heavy's longest gap and capping a constellation that lost 90% of its first satellite to an antenna failure.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

SpaceX is targeting Monday, April 27, 2026 for the next flight of its Falcon Heavy rocket, carrying the ViaSat-3 F3 satellite to a geostationary transfer orbit from Launch Complex 39A at NASA's Kennedy Space Center, [Viasat confirmed](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/20/3277083/0/en/Viasat-Confirms-ViaSat-3-F3-Satellite-to-Launch-April-27-2026.html) in an April 20 press release. The 85-minute launch window opens at 10:21 a.m. EDT.

The mission ends an extended paus...

recommendations: Remove or re-source the April 28 backup window claim
Remove the 'B1098' center core serial unless a source can be cited; alternative: write 'the center core' without the tail number
Correct 'Landing Zones 40 and 2' to 'Landing Zones 1 and 2' (the actual SpaceX RTLS pads at the Cape) — Spaceflight Now's 'LZ-40' is a typo, since SLC-40 is a launch complex, not a landing zone

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong News piece (~771 words, in range). Well-structured: Overview / What Is Launching / Booster Configuration / A Troubled Constellation / What We Don't Know. Tone is appropriately analytical, with measured language about Falcon Heavy's gap and the F1 mishap. The 560-day calculation between Europa Clipper (Oct 14, 2024) and ViaSat-3 F3 (Apr 27, 2026) is mathematically correct.
editor_notes.source_verification: Read all 5 local snapshots from sources/2026-04/falcon-heavy-returns-after-560-days-to-close-out-the-troubled-viasat-3-constellation/ (all 200 OK, no fallbacks). source-0.html (Viasat press release / GlobeNewswire): 10 of 12 attributed claims VERIFIED. Date (April 27 2026), pad (LC-39A KSC), window (85 min, 10:21 a.m. EDT), Falcon Heavy, 1+ Tbps APAC throughput, customer mix, Dankberg quote, multi-orbit architecture, electric propulsion to GEO, late-summer 2026 service — all confirmed. NOT FOUND: April 28 backup window. Minor: 'fixed-broadband' is paraphrase of 'fixed services'. source-1.html (Spaceflight Now launch schedule): B1072 (2nd flight) and B1075 (22nd flight) VERIFIED; center core 'will be expended' VERIFIED; B1098 serial NOT in snapshot; 'Landing Zone 2 and Landing Zone 40' present verbatim, but LZ-40 is a Spaceflight Now typo (no LZ-40 exists at the Cape; actual pads are LZ-1 and LZ-2). source-2.html (Spaceflight Now Atlas 5 / Nov 13 2025): VERIFIED — Atlas 5 551 launch, one-week delay, faulty LOX vent valve replacement, 1+ Tbps Ka-band over the Americas. source-3.html (SpaceNews ViaSat-3 F1): VERIFIED — April 30 2023 launch on Falcon Heavy, antenna failure, >90% capacity loss, $421M insurance claim, limited commercial service plans, replacement-and-relocate plan (minor caveat: source doesn't explicitly name 'F2 or F3' as the trigger, but the constellation plan implies it). source-4.html (SpaceNews Europa Clipper): VERIFIED — Oct 14 2024 launch from LC-39A KSC, fully expendable Falcon Heavy profile.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Three corrections needed: (a) the April 28 backup window is not in the cited Viasat press release, so it must be re-sourced or removed; (b) the center core serial 'B1098' is not in the Spaceflight Now snapshot, so it is unsourced as written; (c) 'Landing Zone 40' inherits a Spaceflight Now typo — the correct designation is LZ-1 and LZ-2 (SLC-40 is a launch pad, not a landing zone). All other facts hold up against the snapshots.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. Frames Viasat's claims (1 Tbps throughput, in-orbit testing schedule, late-summer service) as company-disclosed; uses 'roughly 6-tonne-class' to acknowledge unverified launch mass; closes with hedged language about F1 reshuffle outcomes. No editorializing or AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of Falcon Heavy's return, ViaSat-3 F3, or the broader ViaSat-3 constellation status. Does not duplicate any recent space coverage in 2026-04 (which includes Blue Origin New Glenn reuse, GPS III completion, JWST 29 Cygni b, Hermeus — all distinct topics).
concerns: Three specific factual issues must be addressed before publication: backup window attribution, B1098 center core serial sourcing, and the LZ-40 inherited typo.

The article assumes Falcon Heavy's flight count will rise to 12 if the launch succeeds — that count depends on whether one is counting the demo flight in 2018; this is widely-accepted but not explicitly sourced in the snapshots reviewed.
'Roughly 6-tonne-class communications satellite' for ViaSat-3 F3 is plausible but not in the press release; consider sourcing or removing the specific mass estimate.
recommendations: Find a source naming the April 28 backup window (e.g., a SpaceX or Cape Canaveral range schedule) and re-attribute, or remove the sentence.
Locate the source for B1098 as the center core serial. If it is not publicly reported, drop the tail number and write 'the center core' without the ID.
Fix the Landing Zone designation to LZ-1 and LZ-2 — and consider noting parenthetically that Spaceflight Now's text mistakenly refers to 'LZ-40'.
Optional: tighten 'fixed-broadband' to 'fixed services' to match the press release exactly.
Optional: consider sourcing or removing the 'roughly 6-tonne-class' mass estimate and the Falcon Heavy 'flight count rising to 12' claim.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively a strong piece — most facts (launch parameters, ViaSat-3 F1 mishap, F2 ULA recovery, Europa Clipper anchor) are well-sourced and accurate against the snapshots. Three discrete attribution/sourcing problems and one inherited factual error keep this short of approval. Once those are corrected, this is a clean publish.
